voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Madagascar — tropical rainforest canopy in the Masoala Peninsula
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: It smells scent marks on branches to know which animals have passed through.
It listens for alarm calls and responds quickly to guttural barking from others.
Its forward-facing eyes give sharp focus for judging distances between branches.
It uses smell to assess the ripeness of fruit before eating it.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in the mature rainforest canopy of northeastern Madagascar.
Large, fruit-bearing trees provide most of its food through the year.
Dense canopy gives it hiding spots from aerial predators like hawks.
Its home range must be large because fruiting trees ripen at different times.
How does it send messages?: It gives a loud, harsh barking call to warn the group of predators.
It uses scent glands near its rear to mark territory boundaries on branches.
A roar-shriek chorus with other group members tells neighboring groups a territory is occupied.
Vocalizations while foraging help scattered group members stay in contact.
What does it do when something changes?: It gives alarm calls immediately when it detects a predator overhead.
The group moves quietly and close together after a nearby threat.
It retreats to dense canopy and reduces movement after a disturbance.
It adjusts foraging timing when fruit availability or threat level changes.
How does it learn?: Infants stay in the nest and gradually learn to follow the mother.
Young lemurs travel behind adults and observe which trees hold ripe fruit.
Alarm calls are recognized and responded to from very early in life.
How does it have babies?: The female builds a nest high in the canopy above the forest floor.
She gives birth to two or three infants after about 102 days of gestation.
Other adults in the group help guard the nest while the mother forages.
Infants cannot cling and stay in the nest until mobile.
What are its limits?: Deforestation in Madagascar has destroyed most of its rainforest habitat.
It cannot survive in degraded or fragmented forest with few fruiting trees.
Its slow reproduction — litters only once a year — makes population recovery difficult.
It is listed as Critically Endangered due to severe habitat loss and hunting.
What does this teach us?: It must know where every fruiting tree is and when each one ripens.
Community nest-guarding shows that group members share the cost of raising young.
Its survival depends on one small forest peninsula in Madagascar.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Endemic to a single region in northeastern Madagascar (Masoala Peninsula).
Critically endangered with habitat loss and hunting as leading drivers.
Relies on large, mature fruiting trees that ripen at different times of year.
Uses roar-shriek choruses that coordinate between groups, not just within.
still_debated: How much of the 'territory' signaling is about space vs. access to specific fruiting trees.
Extent of cooperative infant guarding across unrelated adults.
Precise effect of fruit-crop failure years on reproductive success.
common_failure_modes: Confusing its range with the much wider range of the black-and-white ruffed lemur.
Reporting captive litter sizes as if they match wild outcomes.
Calling it 'endangered' when the IUCN listing is 'critically endangered.'
species_specific_guardrails: Always use the correct conservation status: Critically Endangered.
Do not generalize Masoala-specific ecology to all of Madagascar.
Do not claim identical behavior to the black-and-white ruffed lemur.
